content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

There are companies that still say they are evaluating how to adopt artificial intelligence, but the truth is that artificial intelligence is already working for them. It did not enter through the orderly review of an executive committee, nor through the formal architecture of a carefully designed transformation. It entered through another door: through people’s desire not to fall behind, to deliver faster, to write better, to analyze with less friction, to look more capable, and to survive a workload that many times the company itself made unnecessarily heavy.
It entered through the employee who needed to write a clearer email, summarize meeting notes, analyze a customer database, prepare a financial presentation, review a contract, write a formula, understand a document, or simply unblock a task that used to take hours.
And many companies know it, even if they do not want to look at it directly. They turn a blind eye because, for now, the benefit is convenient: more speed, better deliverables, less friction, lower apparent cost, and employees solving problems with tools the organization did not have to buy, implement, govern, or train anyone to use. The problem is that this invisible productivity also brings invisible risk. Because if no one knows what tool is being used, what data went in, what information came out, where it was stored, and under what conditions it was processed, then the company…
